perturbation.original_text: IAC shall cure any membership disruption within six (6) weeks time from the date of its occurrence.
perturbation.changed_text: IAC shall cure any membership disruption within ten (10) weeks time from the date of its occurrence.
perturbation.explanation: Extended the time period to cure membership disruption. This introduces a potential conflict with laws requiring prompt corrective action.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaying corrective action related to EICC membership (which addresses labor standards) could be seen as failing to bargain in good faith if it impacts employees' working conditions or representation. The NLRA requires employers to bargain in good faith with employee representatives, and a significant disruption in ethical labor practices could trigger this obligation. 10 weeks would be considered too long.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: and shall include any individual whose work has ceased as a consequence of, or in connection with, any current labor dispute or because of any unfair labor practice, and who has not obtained any other regular and substantially equivalent employment, but shall not include any individual employed as an agricultural laborer, or in the domestic service of any family or person at his home, or any individual employed by his parent or spouse, or any individual having the status of an independent contractor, or any individual employed as a supervisor, or any individual employed by an employer subject to the Railway Labor Act [45 U.S.C. § 151 et seq.], as amended from time to time, or by any other person who is not an employer as herein defined. (4) The term "representatives" includes any individual or labor organization. (5) The term "labor organization" means any organization of any kind, or any agency or employee representation committee or plan, in which employees participate and which exists for the purpose, in whole or in part, of dealing with employers concerning grievances, labor disputes, wages, rates of pay, hours of employment, or conditions of work. (6) The term "commerce" means trade, traffic, commerce, transportation, or communication among the several States, or between the District of Columbia or any Territory of the United States and any State or other Territory, or between any foreign country and any State, Territory, or the District of Columbia, or within the District of Columbia or any Territory, or between points in the same State but through any other State or any Territory or the District of Columbia or any foreign country. (7) The term "affecting commerce" means in commerce, or burdening or obstructing commerce or the free flow of commerce, or having led or tending to lead to a labor dispute burdening or obstructing commerce or the free flow of commerce. (8) The term "unfair labor practice" means any unfair labor practice listed in section 8 [section 158 of this title]. (9) The term "labor dispute" includes any controversy concerning terms, tenure or conditions of employment, or concerning the association or representation of persons in negotiating, fixing, maintaining, changing, or seeking to arrange terms or conditions of employment, regardless of whether the disputants stand in the proximate relation of employer and employee. (10) The term "National Labor Relations Board" means the National Labor Relations Board provided for in section 3 of this Act [section 153 of this title]. (11) The term "supervisor" means any individual having authority, in the interest of the employer, to hire, transfer, suspend, lay off, recall, promote, discharge, assign, reward, or discipline other employees, or responsibly to direct them, or to adjust their grievances, or effectively to recommend such action, if in connection with the foregoing the exercise of such authority is not of a merely routine or clerical nature, but requires the use of independent judgment. (12) The term "professional employee" means-- (a) any employee engaged in work (i) predominantly intellectual and varied in character as opposed to routine mental, manual, mechanical, or physical work; (ii) involving the consistent exercise of discretion and judgment in its performance; (iii) of such a character that the output produced or the result accomplished cannot be standardized in relation to a given period of time; (iv) requiring knowledge of an advanced type in a field of science or learning customarily acquired by a prolonged course of specialized intellectual instruction and study in an institution of higher learning or a hospital, as distinguished from a general academic education or from an apprenticeship or from training in the performance of routine mental, manual, or physical processes; or (b) any employee, who (i) has completed the courses of specialized intellectual instruction and study described in clause (iv) of paragraph (a), and (ii) is performing re
perturbation.justification: The modification extends the time to cure membership disruption from six to ten weeks, creating a potential conflict with labor laws that require prompt corrective action. The Law Explanation states this delay could be seen as failing to bargain in good faith under the NLRA if it impacts employees' working conditions. Although the provided snippets do not explicitly define 'prompt,' they reference the National Labor Relations Act (NLRA) and terms like 'unfair labor practice' and 'labor dispute' which suggest a legal framework where delays impacting labor standards could be problematic. The Perturbation Explanation indicates this extension could violate federal or state law, thus creating an Inconsistency Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Payment terms are [*] from the date of acceptance by Sonos of an applicable invoice from IAC in accordance with Exhibit H and/or other applicable SOW, which acceptance may not be unreasonably withheld.
perturbation.changed_text: Payment terms are [*] from the date of acceptance by Sonos of an applicable invoice from IAC in accordance with Exhibit H and/or other applicable SOW, which acceptance may not be unreasonably withheld.  However, at Sonos' discretion, payment may be delayed by an additional 90 days.
perturbation.explanation: Added a clause that allows Sonos to arbitrarily delay payments, violating prompt payment laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law requires timely payment to contractors and subcontractors. An additional 90-day delay at Sonos' discretion violates the spirit and potentially the letter of prompt payment laws, especially if no legitimate dispute exists concerning the invoice.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1100 - 22355] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1981, Ch. 306. ) PART 1. ADMINISTRATIVE PROVISIONS [1100 - 9204] ( Heading of Part 1 added by Stats. 1982, Ch. 1120, Sec. 2. ) CHAPTER 7. Contract Clauses [7100 - 7203] ( Chapter 7 added by Stats. 1982, Ch. 1120, Sec. 4. ) 7107. (a) This section is applicable with respect to all contracts entered into on or after January 1, 1993, relating to the construction of any public work of improvement. (b) The retention proceeds withheld from any payment by the public entity from the original contractor, or by the original contractor from any subcontractor, shall be subject to this section. (c) Within 60 days after the date of completion of the work of improvement, the retention withheld by the public entity shall be released. In the event of a dispute between the public entity and the original contractor, the public entity may withhold from the final payment an amount not to exceed 150 percent of the disputed amount. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a clause allowing Sonos to delay payments by an additional 90 days at its discretion. This contradicts the principle of timely payment to contractors, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on public works contracts and retention proceeds, and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is irrelevant, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation strongly suggest that this modification violates prompt payment laws, creating an inconsistency with legal requirements for timely compensation.

perturbation.original_text: Confidentiality shall be maintained for a period of [*] after expiration of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidentiality shall be maintained for a period of one (1) year after expiration of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reduced the standard confidentiality period to one year.  This could be unreasonably short depending on the type of confidential information and industry practices.
perturbation.law_explanation: While CUTSA doesn't specify a required confidentiality period, it protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. A one-year confidentiality period may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, especially given the product development cycles in electronics manufacturing.  If the confidential information continues to provide a competitive advantage beyond one year, the contractual limitation could be challenged as unreasonable and unenforceable, conflicting with the intent of CUTSA to protect trade secrets.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text reduces the confidentiality period to one year, which the Law Explanation suggests may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under CUTSA. While Snippet 1 does not specify a mandatory confidentiality period, it indicates that the Uniform Trade Secrets Act aims to protect trade secrets. By limiting confidentiality to one year, the contract potentially undermines the protection afforded by CUTSA if the trade secret retains its value and competitive advantage beyond that period. This creates an inconsistency with the purpose of trade secret law.
